items.title: Retire contaminated public coding benchmarks before they become release theater
items.why: Once models memorize public tasks or tests reject valid fixes, score deltas stop measuring real engineering ability and start measuring dataset quirks or prior exposure.
how_to_steps: Audit the unsolved slice of your benchmark with experienced engineers and label narrow tests, wide tests, ambiguous specs, and environment-sensitive failures.
Run contamination probes that check whether models can recall gold patches or task-specific implementation details from minimal hints.
Stop using a benchmark in release gates or routing decisions once flawed scoring or memorization materially affects the headline score.
Shift ongoing evaluation toward less exposed or privately authored tasks and keep canary strings plus training-data filters in place for anything public.
items.evidence: OpenAI reports that at least 59.4% of the reviewed hard failures in SWE-bench Verified had material test or specification issues, and that all frontier models it checked showed some contamination from prior exposure to benchmark materials.
items.try_today: Take the coding benchmark score you cite most often, review the first 20 recent misses with a human, and see how many are actually broken tasks or contaminated solves before you trust the next leaderboard jump.
